coredata.dc:title: Influence of dissolved gases on sonochemistry and sonoluminescence in a flow reactor
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gielen, B.
Marchal, S.
Jordens, J.
Thomassen, L.C.J.
Braeken, L.
Van Gerven, T.
coredata.dc:description:
In the present work, the influence of gas addition is investigated on both sonoluminescence (SL) and radical formation at 47 and 248kHz. The frequencies chosen in this study generate two distinct bubble types, allowing to generalize the conclusions for other ultrasonic reactors. In this case, 47kHz provides transient bubbles, while stable ones dominate at 248kHz. For both bubble types, the hydroxyl radical and SL yield under gas addition followed the sequence: Ar>Air>N2 >>CO2. A comprehensive interpretation is given for these results, based on a combination of thermal gas properties, chemical reactions occurring within the cavitation bubble, and the amount of bubbles. Furthermore, in the cases where argon, air and nitrogen were bubbled, a reasonable correlation existed between the OH-radical yield and the SL signal, being most pronounced under stable cavitation at 248kHz. Presuming that SL and OH
originate from different bubble populations, the results indicate that both populations respond similarly to a change in acoustic power and dissolved gas. Consequently, in the presence of non-volatile pollutants that do not quench SL, sonoluminescence can be used as an online tool to qualitatively monitor radical formation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Gases
Sonoluminescence
Radical formation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup consisting of jacketed storage vessel (1) and an ultrasonic flow cell (2) with ultrasonic transducer (3). Circulation through the setup is provided by a centrifugal pump (4). Generator and amplifier are used to generate the ultrasonic field. Gas is supplied to the solution via a sparger submerged in the storage vessel. The photon counting head (5) quantifies the sonoluminescence yield.

Bubble volume in the flow cell after saturation with different gases, measured with the capillary technique. Frequency is set at 248kHz with an acoustic power of 7W. All measurements are performed 9 times using a sonication time of 15s, with the average and the standard deviation being displayed.

Radical yield, displayed as HTA concentration after 60min, as a function of the acoustic power for different gases. Frequency is set at 47kHz and transient cavitation is obtained. All measurements are performed twice with the average and standard deviation being displayed. Solid lines only aid to detect the trend and do not represent any measurements or model.

Radical yield, displayed as HTA concentration after 30min, as a function of the acoustic power for different gases. Frequency is set at 248kHz and stable cavitation is obtained. All measurements are performed twice with the average and standard deviation being displayed. Solid lines only aid to detect the trend and do not represent any measurements or model.

Sonoluminescence yield, displayed as amount of photons per gate time, as a function of the acoustic power for different gases. Frequency is set at 47kHz and transient cavitation is obtained. All measurements are performed nine times with the average and standard deviation being displayed. Solid lines only aid to detect the trend and do not represent any measurements or model.

Sonoluminescence yield, displayed as amount of photons per gate time, as a function of the acoustic power for different gases. Frequency is set at 248kHz and stable cavitation is obtained. All measurements are performed nine times with the average and standard deviation being displayed. Solid lines only aid to detect the trend and do not represent any measurements or model.

Correlation between HTA concentration after 30min and SL yield for different gases. Frequency is set at 248kHz and stable cavitation is obtained. The dashed lines represent a linear fit through the data points.

Correlation between radical formation and sonoluminescence, obtained from literature. (a) is data taken from Kanthale et al., (b) is taken from Beckett et al. [11,30]. The dashed lines represent a linear fit through the data points.

Correlation between HTA concentration after 60min and SL yield for different gases. Frequency is set at 47kHz and transient cavitation is obtained. The dashed lines represent a linear fit through the data points.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated using the solubility data of O2 and N2, with a ratio of 0.21 and 0.79, respectively.

head.ce:title: Influence of dissolved gases on sonochemistry and sonoluminescence in a flow reactor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: KU Leuven, Department of Chemical Engineering, Celestijnenlaan 200 f box 2424, 3001 Leuven, Belgium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: KU Leuven, Faculty of Industrial Engineering, Lab4U, Agoralaan Building B box 8, 3590 Diepenbeek, Belgium

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In the present work, the influence of gas addition is investigated on both sonoluminescence (SL) and radical formation at 47 and 248kHz. The frequencies chosen in this study generate two distinct bubble types, allowing to generalize the conclusions for other ultrasonic reactors. In this case, 47kHz provides transient bubbles, while stable ones dominate at 248kHz. For both bubble types, the hydroxyl radical and SL yield under gas addition followed the sequence: Ar>Air>N2 >>CO2. A comprehensive interpretation is given for these results, based on a combination of thermal gas properties, chemical reactions occurring within the cavitation bubble, and the amount of bubbles. Furthermore, in the cases where argon, air and nitrogen were bubbled, a reasonable correlation existed between the OH-radical yield and the SL signal, being most pronounced under stable cavitation at 248kHz. Presuming that SL and OH
originate from different bubble populations, the results indicate that both populations respond similarly to a change in acoustic power and dissolved gas. Consequently, in the presence of non-volatile pollutants that do not quench SL, sonoluminescence can be used as an online tool to qualitatively monitor radical formation.

ce:section.ce:para:
Ultrasonic reactors show increasing potential for application in chemical and physical processes such as wafer cleaning, waste water treatment and crystallization [1–5]. The beneficial effect of ultrasound arises mostly from acoustic cavitation, involving the formation and implosion of micron-sized bubbles in less than a few microseconds. Depending on the operational conditions, the bubbles can either be stable, oscillating for hundreds of acoustic cycles around their equilibrium radius, or transient when they grow to twice their initial size and implode in a few cycles [6,7]. The implosion of both types of cavitation bubbles creates a local high energy density, giving rise to the formation of a hot spot. As a result, temperature and pressure reach levels of over 5000K and 1000atm in aqueous systems, respectively [8]. These extreme conditions promote the chemical activity of ultrasound, indicated as sonochemistry. The process is initiated by the thermal dissociation of water vapour, yielding reactive hydroxyl (OH
) and hydrogen radicals (H
), whose existence was demonstrated by using electron spin resonance measurements [4,9–11]. Alternatively, the presence of OH
can be indicated by dosimeters such as the hydroxylation of terephthalic acid [4,12,13]. When other species are present in the bubble core, additional radicals and reactive molecules such as HO2
, O
and H2O2 may form. All of these products can recombine, react with other species present in the gas phase of the bubble, or diffuse towards the bulk medium where they can oxidize other compounds. Parallel to these radical reactions, pyrolysis of volatile solutes that evaporate into the bubble takes place [8,14]. In some cases, it was observed that the production of radicals can also promote chemical reactions [15]. In addition, some bubbles can emit light from their core, a phenomenon called sonoluminescence (SL). During the last stages of the adiabatic collapse, short flashes of light, lasting about 100ps, are radiated from the bubble core [16]. In general, this light originates from plasma emissions generated by Bremsstrahlung, excited OH-radicals and other species [17,18]. This was confirmed by SL spectra recordings from multibubble fields, showing characteristic atomic and molecular emission lines from noble gases and active species, superimposed on a broad continuum [19–21]. If the bubbles exhibit a core temperature below 10,000K, emission from electronically and vibrationally excited species mainly causes SL. Similarly, some authors suggest that a bubble cloud is composed of both colder and hotter bubbles, the latter of which generate the broad continuum [17,22]. Moreover, Yasui et al. claim that the exact mechanism of multibubble sonoluminescence (MBSL) depends on the acoustic pressure and frequency. Above 140kHz, the light is mainly caused by plasma emissions other than those originating from excited OH-radicals due to the absence of water vapour in the bubble that normally acts as a source for hydroxyl radicals. The origin of SL at lower frequencies such as 20kHz can be luminescence of OH
or other species, depending on the acoustic pressure. When the amplitude is below 2bar, light emission of the former species is negligible. At higher amplitudes, the bubble contains mostly water vapour, and as a result excited OH-radicals dominate the light emission [17,23]. Pflieger et al. further elucidated the mechanism of sonoluminescence by recording SL spectra of water saturated with different noble gases such as Argon, Krypton and Xenon. By analysis of the OH-emission lines it was shown that a non-thermal plasma was formed in the presence of noble gases during multibubble cavitation [18,21,24].

Overall, sonoluminescence obtained by total light emission measurements or by spectral investigation, gives important information about the processes occurring inside a cavitation bubble [25]. Consequently, this technique is often used to probe the temperature inside the bubble upon collapse [26,27]. Therefore, some authors determine an average bubble temperature Tf [K] using a model that assumes adiabatic compression during the implosion.
(1)
T
f
=
T
i
R
i
R
f
3 ( γ - 1 )

Herein, Ti [K] denotes the initial temperature, Ri [μm] and Rf [μm] the initial and final bubble radius, respectively, and γ [–] the polytropic index of the gas inside the bubble. This equation does not take bubble dynamics and temperature dependence of γ into account, and neglects thermal transport, solvent vapour pressure and chemical reactions. In spite of these simplifications, some experimental results can be reasonably fitted with this model [26]. In contrary, the work of Pflieger et al. questions the use of this average bubble temperature, since it was shown that a non-equilibrium plasma is formed. This type of plasma should be characterized by different temperatures such as the electron (Te ), vibrational (Tv ), rotational (Tr ) and translational temperature (T0 ). Spectral analysis allows to quantify these temperatures, and these usually follow the order: Te
> Tv
> Tr
≈ T0
[18,21,28].

Both sonochemical activity (production of radicals) and sonoluminescence are influenced by operational parameters such as frequency, power, pressure, temperature, addition of gaseous additives, etc. Among these parameters, the effect of frequency and gas addition were proven to be most significant [14].

The ultrasonic frequency determines the active bubble size, which decreases at increasing frequency. Generally, most authors report a more violent bubble collapse at low frequency, characterized by an increased average bubble temperature [14,29,30]. Kanthale et al. studied the effect of frequency on sonoluminescence and H2O2 production. They concluded that both MBSL and sonochemistry depend on the number of active bubbles. In addition, sonoluminescence is influenced by the maximum bubble temperature, while the radical yield is influenced by the average temperature and the amount of water vapour present in the bubbles. The authors reported that the maximum temperature is reduced at increased frequency, resulting in a significant decrease in SL intensity at higher frequency. H2O2 yield showed a similar response in the frequency range between 213 and 647kHz, while a MHz frequency significantly reduced the production of radicals that form H2O2 [30]. Similar results were obtained by Zhou et al. while investigating sonoluminescence and sonochemiluminescence (SCL) at various frequencies. SL signals were also inversely proportional to the ultrasonic frequency, while SCL, a measure for OH-radicals, remained fairly constant from 213 to 647kHz. Only at 1056kHz, the sonochemical intensity dropped significantly [31]. In contrast, recordings of SL spectra at different frequencies between 200kHz and 1.1MHz showed an optimum profile, peaking at 358kHz. Although these results do not show a reduction in SL over the entire frequency domain, as was reported before [30,31], the decline in SL with respect to frequency is again present above 358kHz. The corresponding quantification of the radicals, by H2O2 measurements and degradation kinetics of 1,4-dioxane, showed the same optimum profile as sonoluminescence, with a clear reduction above 1MHz [11]. As was mentioned before, evidence is provided that a non-thermal plasma is created in the bubbles which can be characterized by the electron, vibrational, rotational and translational temperature. Calculation of these plasma temperatures within the frequency range 20–1057kHz showed the opposite effect of the average bubble temperature. Higher electron and vibrational temperatures were obtained at high frequency, meaning that more drastic conditions are created. Therefore, Ndiaye et al. attribute the decrease in total SL intensity at higher frequency that was reported in the previous studies to a lower bubble volume instead of to milder bubble conditions [18]. The lower frequency range from 20kHz to 80kHz was investigated by Hua et al., and it was observed that both H2O2 production and terephthalic acid dosimetry showed identical trends. Sonochemical yield was almost identical at 20 and 40kHz, while a significant increase occurred at 80kHz. The authors attribute this to variations in acoustic pressure, a larger active bubble population, and a reduced collapse time at higher frequency leading to more radical ejection from the bubble [4].

Addition of gas to the solution eases the initiation of cavitation and augments the bubble volume in the reactor [14,32]. The effect of dissolved gases on sonoluminescence and radical formation has been investigated by numerous authors. Young et al. measured SL of water saturated with different gases at a fixed frequency of 20kHz and power density of 10Wcm−2. The SL signal resulted in the following sequence of Ar>Air=O2 >N2 >H2 =CO2 [3]. The attenuation of the SL signal by carbon dioxide was also confirmed by Kumari et al. and Brems et al., both emphasizing the ability of CO2 to control cavitation in order to obtain damage free wafer cleaning. The reduction in sonoluminescence yield was attributed to strong gas diffusion into the bubble, leading to a cushioning effect. In addition, the authors discussed the possibility that carbon dioxide dissolved in water can form carbonic acids that inhibit bubble coalescence and therefore alter the bubble size distribution. As a consequence, the number of collapsing bubbles is strongly reduced [2,33]. The same order for radical production, or at least partially, was obtained in several other studies at different frequencies in the range 300–1000kHz [4,14,29,34–38]. In these different publications, the explanation for the gas sequence is usually based on the gas solubility, the chemical reactions occurring in the bubble, and the average bubble temperature. Using numerical simulations for example, Merouani et al. concluded that the gas solubility and the internal bubble chemistry determine the radical production processes of the bubbles [14,38]. Gas solubility controls the number of collapsing bubbles in the reactor, and a higher value will increase the amount of cavitational collapses. Gases such as O2 and N2 directly influence the radical production, since they participate in reactions that promote or scavenge radicals, respectively. In addition, the bubble temperature is an important factor, as it influences the production rate of OH-radicals. Gases affect the bubble temperature through two aspects. A gas with a higher polytropic index γ yields a higher average bubble temperature, as can be derived from Eq. (1). In turn, an elevated temperature enhances the thermolysis of water molecules, resulting in more radicals. Furthermore, when the gas has a low thermal conductivity, the increase in temperature and sequential radical yield is further facilitated. Although this suggests that radical yield is proportional to the bubble temperature, Merouani et al. claim that an optimum occurs due to the consumption of OH
at high temperature [29]. A study by Okitsu et al. also contradicts the positive correlation between average bubble temperature and radical yield. They report experimental evidence that the average bubble temperature remains constant among different gases, and can thus not influence the radical yield. Instead, it is reported that the chemical activity is in proportion to the solubility of the gas, and is closely related to the number of active bubbles [39]. Other controversial results were reported on the sequence of radical yield among the different gases. The order Ar>Air=O2 >N2 >H2 =CO2 is therefore not generally accepted. Particularly, differences between argon and other polyatomic gases were obtained. Some authors even showed that O2 yields a higher sonochemical activity compared to argon, since it undergoes self-decomposition in the bubble, leading to additional production of radicals [11,29,40–44]. This observation was also confirmed by Pflieger et al. who showed that oxygen dissociation progresses most efficient at higher frequency, as an increased H2O2 yield was measured [45].

Overall, it is clear that cavitation results in sonoluminescence and sonochemical activity, which are significantly influenced by frequency and gas addition. However, the effect of both parameters is not completely understood as contradicting results on the sequence of the gases are reported. Also, there is discussion on the mechanism of saturating gases influencing the sonochemical activity. Moreover, most authors either focus on frequencies below, or above 200kHz. As these frequency ranges can yield two distinct bubble types, i.e. stable and transient, it is important to investigate both ranges in a single setup. Furthermore, it is important to determine in which circumstances sonoluminescence and radical production are correlated as this can establish important insights into the mechanism of SL. A correlation can already be observed in the results of some authors, though no further discussion is provided on the exploitation of this correlation [22,30,31,46–49]. Therefore, this study provides a thorough investigation on the effect of gases and frequency on both the radical yield and sonoluminescence in a single setup. Two frequencies yielding different bubble types will be evaluated in order to investigate which gas sequence is obtained under stable and transient cavitation. This allows for a more general conclusion on the effect of gas addition, independent of the configuration of the ultrasonic reactor. In addition, the correlation between SL and OH-radical yield is discussed. This allows to determine under which circumstances only one of these two variables needs to be measured as they provide similar information. This way, SL measurements could be used to estimate the radical yield, providing an online monitoring tool for ultrasonic degradation processes.

Aqueous terephthalic acid solutions were prepared in a sodium phosphate buffer of pH 7.4, according to a procedure described by Barati et al. [13] The final solution contained 2mM terephthalic acid, 5mM NaOH, 4.4mM NaH2PO4 and 7mM Na2HPO4. Ultrapure water (Milli-Q, resistance 18.2MΩcm), provided by a Sartorius-Stedim Arium 611 DI water purification system was used to dissolve these reagents. The temperature of the solutions was kept constant at 24±2°C throughout the experiment.

To determine the bubble volume, a Pyrex capillary cell was partially submerged in the reactor and mounted on a graduated stand. Due to capillary forces, the liquid retained a fixed level outside the reactor. By application of ultrasound, the liquid level in the capillary increased to a new equilibrium value. A sonication time of 15s was used to determine this new liquid level. Subsequently, it was checked whether the pressure of the ultrasonic sound wave, the presence of bubbles, or a combination of both caused this increase. Therefore, the solution was completely degassed by use of a membrane contactor (Liqui-cel 2.5×8 X40), and the capillary height was measured upon sonication. Due to the absence of gas, no cavitation bubbles were formed at the power levels used in this study. Also, no significant rise in liquid level was observed, indicating that the change in liquid level and the corresponding volume ΔVT is a measure of the total bubble volume created by the ultrasonic field. This was also observed in previous reports, claiming that larger bubbles which are produced from multiple coalescence events of smaller oscillating bubbles are responsible for the volume change [50,51].

Sonoluminescence recordings were repeated three times throughout an experiment. During each measurement, the light intensity was recorded 30s with a gate time of 100ms, using a photon counting head (Hamamatsu, H11890 series). Averaging over this time period corresponds to thousands of acoustic cycles and provides highly reliable data [31]. This detector is able to capture light within the range 230–700nm, but as it is placed outside the glass reactor, the UV range is largely absorbed by the glass. This way, the detector is possible to capture light from around 350nm and above.

The total amount of radicals generated by ultrasound was quantified using terephthalic acid dosimetry [12]. Reaction between terephthalic acid (TA) and hydroxyl radicals, produced by ultrasound, yields 2-hydroxyl terephthalic acid (HTA) that can be measured using fluorescence spectrophotometry. During each run, 300mL of a 2mM TA solution was circulated through the setup under continuous sonication. At fixed time intervals, 5mL samples were taken from the vessel and kept refrigerated in complete darkness to prevent spontaneous formation of HTA. All samples were stored for maximum 48h, prior to analysis. Next, the fluorescence intensity was measured using a spectrofluorimeter (Edinburgh Instruments FS900) with an excitation wavelength of 312nm, and emission maximum at 425nm. A calibration curve of HTA was prepared between 0.5 and 10μM, resulting in a linear response with an R 2-value above 0.95. Samples taken from the reactor had a concentration within the range of this calibration curve, and could thus be directly measured without any dilution or pre-treatment steps.

Fig. 1 shows the experimental setup, consisting of a jacketed vessel of which the content is continuously recycled over a glass flow cell (10×10×55mm) by a micro centrifugal pump (TCS Micropumps M400). The flowrate is kept constant at 650mLmin−1 in all experiments. The ultrasonic transducer (M.P. Interconsulting, type MPI-DISK-SPZT8-500X35) was glued to the outer wall of the flow reactor, and could be operated at 47kHz or 248kHz. The cavitation type for each frequency was determined in the same reactor in a previous paper [52]. At 47kHz it generated transient cavitation bubbles, while stable ones were obtained at 248kHz. A generator (Picotest G5100), connected to an amplifier (E&I 1020L RF), supplied the sinusoidal signal to the transducer in order to generate and control the ultrasonic field. Calorimetric power levels, varying from 0.8 to 2.5W at 47kHz and 0.8 to 7W at 248kHz, were used during the experiments. Gases argon, carbon dioxide, nitrogen, each with a purity of over 99.99%, were introduced in the solution using a sintered metal sparger that was submerged in the jacketed vessel. Prior to an experiment, the solution was circulated for at least 15min through the setup without sonication under continuous addition of gas. This gas flow was maintained during the experiment to guarantee complete saturation of the solution.

Fig. 2 shows the results of the capillary method to determine the bubble volume while the reactor was operated at a frequency of 248kHz using an acoustic power of 7W. Four different gases were studied, argon, air, nitrogen and carbon dioxide, all showing a bubble volume in the nano-litre scale. Carbon dioxide exhibits a bubble volume one order of magnitude higher in comparison with the other gases. This can be attributed to the higher solubility of carbon dioxide compared to the other gases, as shown in Table 1
[53–55]. The bubble volume measurements appear to follow the same trend as the gas solubility. This observation was also reported in other work [51]. Only air slightly deviates as its bubble volume closely matches the value of argon which is not expected based on the solubility data. Both nitrogen and oxygen in air can result in bubble formation. However, it is expected that oxygen can initiate the bubble formation more easily compared to nitrogen, as was observed in previous reports [8,34–36]. Oxygen thus dictates the bubble generation in an air-saturated solution, and the bubble volume therefore corresponds closely to the solubility of oxygen. In general, the results indicate that saturation with a more soluble gas yields a higher bubble volume. This does not directly coincide with higher sonochemical activity, as not all bubbles are equally active in the sound field. In order for cavitation and its arising effects such as sonoluminescence and radical formation to occur, the bubble size and core temperature should correspond to a particular range. Hence, bubbles larger than the resonance bubble size or with a low bubble core temperature will produce substantially less light and radicals. The results presented in Fig. 2 thus do not allow to directly extract information on the effect of these gases on the sonochemical activity. In contrary, it shows that highly soluble gases (e.g. CO2) that yield a large number of bubbles in the reactor do not necessarily improve sonochemical activity and sonoluminescence yield.

Figs. 3
and 4 show the 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA) concentration after 60min as a function of the ultrasonic power for the different gases at 47kHz and 248kHz, respectively. This dosimetry method allows to estimate the hydroxyl radical yield in the solution. In these set of experiments, a 300mL 0.2mM terephthalic acid solution was circulated through the setup under continuous sonication, converting it into HTA through reaction with hydroxyl radicals. At 47kHz, the HTA concentration is displayed after 60min, while a reaction time of 30min is used for the experiments at 248kHz. This unequal time frame was chosen because low frequency ultrasound generally produces less radicals compared to higher frequencies [44]. Hence, to obtain detectable HTA concentrations, the exposure time at 47kHz was doubled with respect to the tests at 248kHz. Still, results show more radical production at 248kHz, even when similar input powers were used.

In general, both frequencies and corresponding bubble types show similar trends. At first, an overall increase in radical yield is obtained at increased power for most gases. This observation is in agreement with results from Kanthale et al., reporting an increased radical production by increasing the input power from 5W to 25W [30]. Other authors observed a similar trend, and attribute this to a higher amount of bubbles, a higher bubble core temperature, or a combination of both [31,49]. The slope is less pronounced at 47kHz, as some curves tend to level off, and the measurements show a larger deviation. Zhou et al. claim that levelling off at higher power is due to bubble coalescence, degassing and clustering [31]. This phenomenon was also reported by Henglein et al. during measurement of iodine production from KI under increasing acoustic power [56,57]. After an initial rise of the iodine yield with power, it reaches a plateau, similar as the results presented in Fig. 3. The authors also attribute this to bubble clustering which occurs at higher intensity, leading to less extreme conditions compared to separated resonance bubbles. Since the bubbles are larger at 47kHz compared to 248kHz, these clustering and coalescence effects are more likely to occur. The larger deviation is attributed to the transient nature of the bubbles at this frequency, as shown by previous measurements [52]. Transient cavitation bubbles have a chaotic oscillation and a short life time of only a couple of acoustic cycles. Due to this rather unpredictable behaviour, they show more experimental deviations as can be seen from Fig. 3. Also, it is observed that the radical yield does not increase for both frequencies when the solution is saturated with carbon dioxide. This confirms the reports from Brems et al. and Kumari et al., claiming that carbon dioxide can be used to suppress cavitation in a solution.[2,33] Furthermore, the radical yield obtained with different gases shows the same sequence at every power level for both frequencies: argon, air, nitrogen, and finally carbon dioxide. This order is more remarkable at the higher frequency, which confirms the observations of Merouani et al. [14]. In order to interpret these results, the thermal properties of the gases, the specific chemical reactions taking place upon implosion, and the amount of active bubbles need to be taken into account.

The thermal properties of the gases are displayed in Table 1, including the polytropic index γ and the thermal conductivity λ. Argon has the highest polytropic index, followed by air and nitrogen, and finally carbon dioxide. An opposite trend is observed for the thermal conductivity. Both properties influence the average bubble temperature, and the heat conduction around the core after implosion. This is shown in Eq. (1), where a higher γ will lead to a higher Tf . A solution saturated with argon will thus exhibit the highest temperature as the increased bubble core temperature is maintained in time due to the low thermal conductivity. Similarly, in the presence of CO2 the core temperature is strongly reduced due its low polytropic index and high thermal conductivity. Since earlier reports demonstrated that a higher temperature stimulates radical production, the observed sequence for the gases can be explained using the thermal properties [58–61]. However, this reasoning fails to explain the distinction between air and nitrogen, as both gases have similar thermal characteristics. The chemical reactions occurring at the bubble implosion also provide information to elucidate the observed sequence, and allow to make a distinction between air and nitrogen. Lee et al. and Jerrard et al. showed that dissolved nitrogen reacts to form radical scavengers such as NO2, hence decreasing the hydroxyl radical yield [14,23]. An air-saturated solution thus produces more radicals as it contains less nitrogen, and features oxygen instead which even promotes radical formation. This radical producing effect by O2 was also observed in other reports, and increases with frequency [45]. The complete absence of radical production under carbon dioxide saturation might be attributed to chemical reactions, as was reported by Brems et al. The authors speculated that the presence of CO2 inhibits bubble coalescence, rectified diffusion and sequential bubble growth. As a result, the bubble cannot reach the critical bubble radius to overcome the Blake threshold for cavitation, reducing sonoluminescence and radical formation [2]. However, as already mentioned by the authors, this hypothesis is debatable and the results of the present work contradict this phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 2, a significant volume change is measured after saturation with carbon dioxide. Since this technique measures the bubble volume of stable bubbles after several coalescence events, it is thus unlikely that CO2 hinders bubble coalescence. Therefore, it is expected that reduced SL and OH-radical yield under carbon dioxide saturation is due to its thermal properties. Finally, the amount of active bubbles generated for each gas type might also be used to interpret the radical yield. The total amount of bubbles was estimated earlier, using the capillary method, as illustrated in Fig. 2. As stated above, this technique probes the entire bubble population. The following sequence was obtained for the different gases using this method: CO2 >>Ar⩾Air>N2. A large bubble population thus does not necessarily correlates with a high radical yield, as is clearly noticeable for carbon dioxide. Although its bubble volume exceeds all other gases, no radical production occurs due to the unfavourable conditions related to the thermal properties. Nonetheless, when circumstances are beneficial for radical production, the capillary method gives an indication of the expected radical yield. After all, a larger bubble population has more chance to exhibit more active bubbles than a small one. Taking this into account, the sequence of the bubble measurements for argon, air and nitrogen almost matches the radical yield. A more soluble gas leads to more bubble formation, and when the right conditions are met, these bubbles are active in the sound field and produce radicals. This observation coincides with the work of Chendke et al. who reported a linear relation between H2O2 yield and the quantity of dissolved gas, with CO2 being the outlier [62].

In summary, a combination of the three arguments – the thermal properties, the chemical reactions and the amount of bubbles – allows to explain the radical yield obtained for the different gases. First of all, carbon dioxide inhibits HTA formation due to the fact that the bubble temperature is too low, attributed to a low polytropic index and high thermal conductivity. Air and nitrogen both have similar thermal properties resulting in a comparable bubble temperature. The reduced radical yield under nitrogen saturation is caused by production of radical scavengers and a lower bubble volume. Saturation with argon creates the most favourable conditions for radical production as the bubble core temperature and the amount of bubbles increases by addition of this gas.

Figs. 5
and 6 show the sonoluminescence yield for different gases at 47kHz (transient cavitation) and 248kHz (stable cavitation), respectively, that was obtained simultaneously with the radical measurements. Three sequential recordings were performed at three time periods, and all data were averaged in order to get a representative sonoluminescence (SL) signal for the radical experiments. A similar trend as the radical plots is obtained at both frequencies. Sonoluminescence signals rise at increasing power input, but the plots at low frequency show the tendency to level off at the end.

Also, the amount of photons obtained at 248kHz is one order of magnitude higher compared to 47kHz. A similar observation can be derived from the results of Ndiaye et al. who investigated SL spectra over a broad frequency range [18]. It is shown that the SL spectra at higher frequencies exhibit a higher intensity compared to low frequency, which even allows to differentiate some OH vibrational bands more clearly. Deconvulation of the spectra allowed the authors to determine the vibrational and electron temperature that can be used to characterize the non-thermal plasma that is created upon bubble implosion. Both Te as Tv increased with the ultrasonic frequency, and this rise is most pronounced in the low electron temperature region from 20 to 204kHz. The higher photon yield in the present study at 248kHz compared to 47kHz can thus be explained by the more extreme conditions created during bubble implosion at higher frequency. Furthermore, our results show the same gas sequence for both frequencies and bubble types at every power level. Argon exhibits the highest photon yield, followed by air, nitrogen and carbon dioxide. The reasoning for this sequence is very similar to the one for the radicals, and is again based on the thermal properties, the specific chemical reactions and the amount of bubbles. Gases that lead to more extreme bubble conditions also raise the sonoluminescence yield. As described earlier, the light originates from emissions of several excited species. Gases influencing the average bubble core or vibrational temperature can thus affect both processes. A higher temperature introduces more energy in the bubble content, exciting more species that can generate light upon their subsequent relaxation. For example, an excited water molecule present within the bubble will relax through a transition state, emitting light at around 425nm. When more molecules are excited, a higher light intensity is obtained. In addition, an acceleration or deceleration of the electrons in this plasma, caused by collisions with neutral and positive particles in the bubble, results in a change in electron energy and is associated with broadband Bremstrahlung emission. It is expected that gas bubbles with a higher temperature are more likely to create a plasma and can thus emit light by a change in electron velocity. Interpreting the thermal properties, summarized in Table 1, in a similar way as before, the sequence for light production becomes clear: argon>air≈nitrogen>>carbon dioxide. However, it still does not explain the strong SL enhancement by argon, and the deviation between air and nitrogen. Again, these anomalies should be based on the chemical reactions taking place. Argon is an inert gas that can excite other species such as water molecules, which emit light upon relaxation. Argon also promotes specific light emitting reactions [25]. In the end, the contributions of all these additional excited species and specific reactions yield a strong enhancement of the sonoluminescence signal for argon compared to the other gases. The deviation between oxygen and nitrogen is caused by the radical quenching ability of the latter one. In addition, oxygen takes part in some sonolytic reactions that yield radicals, which becomes more effective at high frequency [45]. These radicals can either by excited, emitting a continuum, or excite other molecules which causes light emission at specific wave lengths. As less radicals are present under nitrogen saturation, a lower SL signal is obtained. Finally, the bubble volume measurements can also be used to explain the sequence in light production. A highly soluble gas produces more bubbles, and when the gas also provides the right conditions such as a high temperature, a larger number of bubbles becomes active and produces light upon implosion. This trend of an increased sonoluminescence activity with increasing amount of dissolved gas was also demonstrated by Chendke et al., confirming our observations [62]. Although the number of bubbles can give an indication for the sequence, it does not allow to explain the clear enhancement obtained under argon saturation.

As a very similar trend was observed for the effect of the different gases on both radical and sonoluminescence yield, the correlation between the two variables was investigated. Fig. 9 plots the obtained radical yield as a function of the sonoluminescence signal for all gases at 248kHz, where stable cavitation is obtained. This approach can show whether sonoluminescence is a good indication for the presence of radicals, allowing to monitor ultrasonic degradation processes.

Under stable cavitation at 248kHz, there is a clear correlation between the sonoluminescence yield and the radical production, expressed as a HTA concentration, for all gases except carbon dioxide. Since there is almost no radical production and light output for this gas, the absence of a correlation seems logical. The plots for argon, air and nitrogen show a highly linear correlation as the R 2-values almost approach unity. We hypothesize that this correlation can be attributed to the origin of sonoluminescence in the presence of stable bubbles. As mentioned before, sonoluminescence is a combination of light originating from excited OH-radicals and several other species. The former ones transmit some characteristic wavelengths in the UV range, along with an emission continuum from 200–400nm [11]. Part of this continuum (350–400nm) will be detected by the photon counting head, together with other emissions up to 700nm. As more radicals are produced by increasing power for each gas type, more light can therefore be emitted. The strong correlation between the amount of radicals and the total light signal suggests that light emitting reactions involving radicals have an important share in the total signal. This can also be observed in the SL spectra of several other studies which show that the highest SL intensity is obtained within the range 200–400nm due to the OH-emissions [11,21]. Another interesting observation from Fig. 7 can be made by a comparison of the slopes of the different gases. Air and nitrogen have a similar slope, meaning that a similar radical concentration yields the same sonoluminescence signal. This can be seen by comparing the radical and SL yield data in Figs. 4 and 6, respectively. The HTA concentration at 2.4W using nitrogen is almost equal to that at 0.8W using air. As a result, a similar SL yield is obtained for both the data points. The same conclusion is possible for the data points at 5.4W and 2.4W, saturated with nitrogen and air, respectively. The fact that air can produce higher SL values is caused by its ability to generate more radicals compared to nitrogen for reasons explained before. The equal slope is probably attributed to the same thermal properties, resulting in similar temperatures in the bubble core and radical production rate. Argon exhibits a smaller slope, and from a certain threshold, a higher sonoluminescence signal is obtained at the same radical concentration compared to air and nitrogen. Two reasons, both based on the more extreme conditions and accompanying temperatures created by the presence of argon, are hypothesized to cause this increase. On the one hand, the radicals produced under argon saturation are more easily excited by the higher temperature and therefore participate more in light emitting reactions. The amount of light generated per OH-radical therefore increases. On the other hand, different light emitting processes occur and these increase the overall light intensity, while the amount of light generated per hydroxyl radical remains constant. Since a strong correlation between hydroxyl radical generation and SL is still present, these additional light emissions should increase as the number of radicals rises.

The OH
-SL correlation was confirmed by plotting results of other publications in literature in a similar way. Fig. 8 shows results taken from Kanthale et al. and Beckett et al., respectively [11,30]. These studies employ hydrogen peroxide and degradation rate of 1,4-dioxane to quantify the radical yield, which are alternatives for the TA dosimetry. The correlation between sonoluminescence and radical formation is again strongly present, demonstrated by relative high R2-values. Data from the former one is obtained under atmospheric conditions, meaning air is present in the solution. The latter study used several argon to oxygen ratios varying from 100% to 0%. In this case, only the data where oxygen was present are used in the plot. The studies were operated at a frequency of 213kHz and 358kHz, respectively, which closely matches the 248kHz, applied in the present work. The dominant bubble type in these studies was, however, not determined.

The correlation between sonoluminescence and radical formation diminishes as bubbles become transient, as shown in Fig. 9 for 47kHz. For the solution saturated with air, the dependency between both parameters even completely disappears. The overall decrease in correlation can be related to the reduced hydroxyl radical yield at this frequency. As shown before, the vibrational and electron temperature inside low frequency bubbles is lower compared to high frequency. This reduces the dissociation of water molecules into hydrogen and hydroxyl radicals. The emission continuum of radicals in the overall SL yield will thus strongly diminish. Consequently, the share of the light emitted by the radical reactions in the total signal therefore reduces, and the SL signal cannot directly be linked to the production of hydroxyl radicals, reducing the correlation. The complete lack of correlation under air saturation is due to the presence of oxygen molecules. On the one hand, the presence of oxygen promotes the generation of other species such as OH2
and OOH
, all participating in light emitting reactions. On the other hand, Pflieger et al. recently showed that the presence of O2 can efficiently quench excited OH-species at low frequency [45]. Both phenomena can strongly reduce the relative importance of the light emitting hydroxyl radical reactions in the total SL signal and hence the correlation.

Despite this discussion on the results, the observed connection between SL and hydroxyl radical yield is still debatable. According to some researchers, production of radicals and sonoluminescence does not always occurs simultaneously. Sunartio et al. studied acoustic cavitation in aqueous solutions containing sodium chloride, and showed the presence of bubbles that were only sonochemically active without producing SL [63]. The results of Lepoint et al. show the same phenomenon during single bubble experiments. They created a single dancing bubble at 24kHz which did not produce any SL, although chemical reaction did occur [64]. Brotchie et al. also investigated this effect by determining the size distributions of bubble that produce sonoluminescence and sonochemiluminescence (SCL). Both bubble populations exhibited a different mean size, indicating that SL and SCL take place in different cavitation bubbles [65]. Taking these studies into account which show that radical and light production do not always occur simultaneously or originate from the same bubble, a correlation between both can only occur under specific circumstances. Therefore, we assume that in the present work, both bubble populations are present in the reactor and show a similar response to varying acoustic pressure and dissolved gas, making a correlation between SL and OH
-yield feasible. Especially under stable cavitation the correlation was most pronounced, indicating that SL and radical measurements are interchangeable. In this case, ultrasonic degradation could be monitored online using SL measurements. Although this technique is not completely quantitative, it will give a good indication on the presence of radicals. However, monitoring can only be used when non-volatile pollutants are present in the solution that do not quench the SL signal or produce light emitting species that mask the OH-emission. Furthermore when volatile pollutants are present, online monitoring will not be possible. These vaporize into the cavitation bubble in order to be degraded, leading to a strong reduction in the SL signal [61,66].

This work evaluated the influence of gas addition on the cavitational activity of both transient and stable bubbles, generated in a flow reactor at 47kHz and 248kHz, respectively. The activity was quantified using bubble volume, sonoluminescence and hydroxyl radical measurements. The latter two were simultaneously recorded in order to study their correlation. All gases – argon, air, nitrogen and carbon dioxide – follow an identical sequential order for both sonoluminescence and radical measurements, independent of the cavitation type. Argon yields the highest signal, followed by air, nitrogen, and finally carbon dioxide. This order is explained taking all factors, i.e. the thermal properties of the gases, the specific reactions occurring upon bubble implosion and the bubble volume measurements, into account. In addition, a correlation is shown between the sonoluminescence signal and hydroxyl radical formation, being most clear under stable cavitation at 248kHz. Considering that two separate bubble populations cause these phenomena, the correlation indicates that these populations show a similar dependency on acoustic power and gas type. This makes is feasible to use SL as a qualitative online monitoring tool in some cases to determine the amount of radicals inside a ultrasonic reactor. However, this technique has a reduced accuracy in the presence of transient bubbles, and volatile pollutants that quench or mask the SL signal can even totally inhibit online monitoring. Overall, this research demonstrates how dissolved gases can control the sonochemical activity, with the possibility for online monitoring using SL in some distinct cases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research leading to these results has received funding from the European Community’s Seventh Framework Programme (FP7/2007-2013) under Grant agreement n° NMP2-SL-2012-309874 (ALTEREGO). J. Jordens acknowledges funding of a Ph.D. grant by the Agency for Innovation by Science and Technology (IWT).
